summary: A delayed stale-lease contender can unlink a successor's fresh owner lease before its own O_EXCL create; O_EXCL does not make the preceding pathname removal a CAS.
Owner heartbeat refresh verifies ownerPid before an unconditional atomic rename, so a replacement between read and rename can be overwritten; no lease generation fences stale writers.
Release preserves daemon discovery metadata, but adopters clear their owner lease and bridge without taking durable supervision, causing later sessions to repeat stale-adoption work.

Session storms multiply readiness probes; the process-lifetime DB lock remains a final safety fence but turns arbitration mistakes into expensive exit-86 retry/bridge cycles rather than causing the probe stall directly.
All five findings stem from implicit context and authority being reconstructed at subsystem boundaries instead of carried through one canonical runtime context and fenced lifecycle transition.

focus: Synthesize lease/re-election and lock-arbitration robustness under concurrent multi-session usage
noveltyJustification: Found a concrete stale-owner unlink/heartbeat fencing race, distinguished ownerless adoption from durable re-election, modeled probe/DB-lock amplification, and produced a unified root cause plus ranked hardening sequence.
nextFocus: Deterministically verify stale-unlink and heartbeat-overwrite races, then assess the smallest election-lock plus leaseId protocol.
